judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently identify the same root cause, fix, and wipe-related failure with nearly identical accuracy and detail; report 2 is marginally more thorough (e.g., explicitly notes cedar_owner is recreated via POSTGRES_USER and suggests folding bootstrap into the reset flow), but both fully satisfy all three expectations.

report_1.evidence: The responsible configuration is `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`): `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`. The application connects as **`cedar_owner`**, which is the role that owns the tables... the owner (and superusers) bypass row-level security policies unless the table is also set to `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`. The migration in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` only enables RLS; it never forces it.

report_2.evidence: `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`) set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`... It uses only `ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`, **not `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`**... Postgres semantics: with plain `ENABLE`, RLS policies are skipped for the table owner (and superusers / `BYPASSRLS` roles). Because the app is that owner, the `tenant_isolation` policy is never applied

report_1.evidence: Point the application at the least-privilege role: change `DATABASE_URL` in `.env`/`.env.example` to connect as **`cedar_app`** (the role from `create_app_role.sql`), which does not own the tables... Reserve `cedar_owner` strictly for running migrations (`migrate.sh`), never for the running app. This is the two-role model: a schema-owning/migration role and a separate least-privilege application role.

report_2.evidence: Point the application at the least-privilege, non-owner role instead of the owner: change `DATABASE_URL` in `.env`/`.env.example` to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`... Structurally, keep a clean split of roles: **DDL/migrations and object ownership** run as the privileged `cedar_owner` (as `migrate.sh` does), while **all application runtime traffic** uses the restricted `cedar_app` role that is subject to RLS.

report_1.evidence: `cedar_app` is created only by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which is a **one-time, manual** step — its own header says it is "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup,"... `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, and the `-v` **destroys that volume**... The reset script then only recreates the container and runs `scripts/migrate.sh` (migrations) — it never re-runs the bootstrap.

report_2.evidence: The `cedar_app` role is created **only** by the one-time bootstrap `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which its own header and `docs/local-setup.md` describe as a manual first-time step ("Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup")... `scripts/reset-local-db.sh` runs `docker compose down -v`, which deletes the `cedar_pgdata` volume... but the reset script never re-runs the role bootstrap. So after a reset, the fresh cluster has the `cedar_owner` superuser (recreated from `POSTGRES_USER`) and the tables, but **no `cedar_app` role**.
